sections.name: Email Body
sections.content:
Hook
It's been about seven weeks since you started.
Maybe you're seeing small shifts, a pause, a look down, a walk toward the bathroom that used to end in a meltdown. Maybe it's slower than you hoped. Either way, here's what I know about where you are right now:
Pre-K is coming.
And you're wondering if he'll be ready.
That question sits heavy. I get it. Both my sons went through this, the calendar ticking while the progress felt like millimeters.
Validation
Here's what I want you to hear first: your child's progress in the last seven weeks is real. Even if it doesn't look like "trained" yet.
That pause he takes when he feels the uh-oh sensation? That's his nervous system registering a signal it didn't receive seven weeks ago. That look down at his pants? That's interoception building, his brain learning to connect the body signal to the awareness.
Research shows autistic children have significantly reduced body awareness, the ability to sense internal body states like bladder fullness (Nicholson et al., 2019, Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders). What you're seeing over these weeks isn't failure. It's a nervous system literally building new pathways.
Pre-K doesn't require perfection. It requires that his teachers understand what's happening, and that you and they are aligned on how to support him.
Mechanism, School Readiness Protocol
Here are three things you can start this week:
1. The teacher conversation template
What to say:
> "My son is autistic. He's learning to connect body signals to the bathroom, but right now the signal arrives AFTER he needs to go. We use BrightKidCo underwear that gives him a gentle sensation so his brain learns. When he has an accident, please just help him change quietly. No shame, no pressure. We're building awareness, not compliance."
2. The pull-up-over-underwear system
Send him to school in a thin pull-up OVER his BrightKidCo underwear. This does two things:
- The pull-up protects against accidents in the classroom
- The underwear underneath still has skin contact, so the body-signal learning continues throughout the school day
Teachers understand pull-ups. They don't understand body-signal layers. Meet them where they are.
3. The morning and after-school rhythm
The school day can dysregulate sensory systems. Build two consistent checkpoints:
- Morning: 10 minutes of calm bathroom time before leaving. No pressure to perform. Just the routine of sitting with his underwear on.
- After-school: 30 minutes in BrightKidCo underwear only (no pull-up) when he gets home. This his body-signal learning window, when his nervous system is decompressing and most receptive.
Every single day of body-signal exposure is building a pathway that wasn't there before. That pause, that look, that single step toward the bathroom — those are the evidence.
CTA
The 3+3 Bundle is still the best place to start if you haven't tried it yet, 6 pairs, free shipping, and the 60-day guarantee. If you already have pairs, one extra set means more learning time between laundry cycles.
[Shop the 3+3 Bundle →]
60 days to try. Full refund if it doesn't work for your child. Your judgment.
Lena Sign-off
With understanding,
Lena ——
Customer Support · Mom of two autistic sons
P.S. If your Pre-K asks about accommodations, reply to this email and I'll send you a one-page teacher info sheet that explains body-signal learning in teacher-friendly language. I send these to every parent who asks.
Footer
You're receiving this because you signed up at brightkidco.com and indicated your child is in the Pre-K window.
[Update preferences] · [Unsubscribe]
BrightKidCo LLC
123 Parent Support Lane
Los Angeles, CA 90001
